skill.name: postgres-best-practices
skill.description: Postgres performance and security guidance for reviewing SQL queries, schema design, indexes, connection settings, RLS policies, and operational diagnostics.
skill.summary: Apply practical Postgres optimization rules for faster, safer database design and query review.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found they are documentation examples, SQL snippets, and reference links rather than executable skill behavior. No scripts, network clients, environment access, dynamic execution, credential exfiltration, or prompt-injection instructions were found. Residual risk is low because the skill includes administrative SQL guidance that users should review before applying to production databases.

low_findings.title: Static command-execution alerts are Markdown SQL examples
low_findings.description: The external command detections occur in fenced Markdown examples and contributor instructions, not executable Ruby or shell source. README.md shows npm commands for contributors, while AGENTS.md shows SQL query examples inside code fences.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are documentation code fences, and the repository inventory found no JavaScript, shell, Ruby, Python, or package files that would execute them as part of the skill.

low_findings.title: Static script alerts are PostgreSQL COPY options
low_findings.description: The with-statement detections are SQL COPY syntax using WITH options, not JavaScript with statements or script execution. The same pattern appears in the compiled guide and the source rule for batch inserts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are inside SQL examples for bulk loading data. They do not introduce scope confusion or any executable scripting behavior in the skill runtime.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs are documentation references
low_findings.description: The network detections are links to PostgreSQL, Supabase, and related documentation. They are static references in Markdown and metadata, with no code that fetches from those URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The locations contain plain reference URLs. No fetch, curl, wget, HTTP client, or other outbound network execution path was found in the skill files.

low_findings.title: Weak-crypto alerts are terminology false positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm detections are caused by words such as hash, critical, and documentation anchors in Postgres guidance. The examples discuss hash indexes and hashtext advisory locks, not password hashing or cryptographic primitives.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding context is database indexing and lock documentation. I did not find cryptographic implementation code or guidance to use weak algorithms for security purposes.

low_findings.title: Operational SQL examples require user review
low_findings.description: The skill includes database administration examples such as ALTER SYSTEM, grants, revokes, and COPY from a local path. These are legitimate for a Postgres best-practices skill, but users should review environment impact before applying them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples can affect database configuration, permissions, or imported data if copied directly. This is an operational safety concern, not evidence of malicious intent.

content.value_statement: Slow queries and unsafe database patterns are hard to catch during ordinary code review. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code structured Postgres guidance for indexes, RLS, pooling, transactions, and diagnostics.
actual_capabilities: Reviews SQL queries for missing indexes, inefficient filters, and poor join patterns.
Recommends B-tree, GIN, BRIN, hash, composite, covering, and partial indexes by query shape.
Guides schema choices for data types, foreign key indexes, partitioning, primary keys, and identifiers.
Explains connection pooling, connection limits, idle timeouts, and prepared statement tradeoffs.
Improves RLS policies, privilege grants, transaction scope, locking patterns, and queue processing.
Uses monitoring patterns such as pg_stat_statements, VACUUM, ANALYZE, and EXPLAIN ANALYZE.
limitations: It provides guidance, not live database profiling or automatic query plan collection.
Recommendations must be checked against production workload, data volume, and Postgres version.
Some examples require database administrator privileges and should not be copied blindly.
It focuses on Postgres and Supabase patterns, not every ORM or database engine.

use_cases.title: Review a slow query before release
use_cases.description: Check filters, joins, pagination, and selected columns against Postgres indexing rules before merging application code.

use_cases.title: Design a scalable tenant schema
use_cases.description: Plan primary keys, RLS policies, foreign key indexes, partitioning, and data types for a multi-tenant product.

use_cases.title: Improve Supabase operational settings
use_cases.description: Review connection pooling, idle timeouts, prepared statements, and monitoring queries for a growing Supabase workload.

prompt_templates.title: Check one query
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Postgres query for performance problems. Identify missing indexes, inefficient filters, and safer alternatives. Explain each recommendation briefly.

prompt_templates.title: Review a schema migration
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Postgres migration for data type choices, primary keys, foreign key indexes, naming, and future scaling risks. Suggest precise migration changes.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize RLS policies
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these Supabase RLS policies for security and performance. Check function calls, indexes, policy scope, and least-privilege access.

prompt_templates.title: Create a production tuning plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a prioritized Postgres tuning plan from these queries, schema notes, and workload constraints. Include indexes, pooling, transactions, monitoring, and rollout risks.

output_examples.input: A query filters orders by status and created date but only has separate single-column indexes.
output: Recommend a composite index with equality columns before range columns.
Explain that separate indexes may require a bitmap scan and more heap access.
Suggest validating with EXPLAIN ANALYZE on realistic data.

output_examples.input: A Supabase policy compares auth.uid() directly to a user_id column on a large table.
output: Recommend wrapping auth.uid() in a SELECT expression so it is evaluated once per statement.
Confirm an index exists on the user_id column used by the policy.
Flag complex checks for review with a security definer helper function.

output_examples.input: A worker queue selects the oldest pending job with FOR UPDATE and multiple workers block each other.
output: Recommend FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED for non-blocking job claims.
Prefer a single claim-and-update statement that returns the selected job.
Keep the transaction short to reduce lock contention.

best_practices: Validate recommendations with EXPLAIN ANALYZE and representative production-like data.
Prioritize indexes for frequent WHERE, JOIN, ORDER BY, and RLS policy columns.
Review administrative SQL in a staging environment before applying it to production.
anti_patterns: Adding separate single-column indexes for every multi-column query without checking column order.
Using application-only tenant filters instead of database-enforced RLS policies.
Keeping transactions open while external API calls or long application work runs.

faq.question: Does this skill run SQL against my database?
faq.answer: No. It provides review guidance and examples. You decide what to run in your environment.

faq.question: Is it specific to Supabase?
faq.answer: It includes Supabase RLS and pooling guidance, but most query and schema advice applies to Postgres.

faq.question: Can it replace EXPLAIN ANALYZE?
faq.answer: No. Use it to form hypotheses, then verify plans and timings with EXPLAIN ANALYZE.

faq.question: Does it cover security?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes least privilege, RLS basics, and RLS performance patterns.

faq.question: Can beginners use it?
faq.answer: Yes. The prompts can ask for short explanations and concrete migration or query changes.

faq.question: What should I review before applying examples?
faq.answer: Check privileges, lock impact, data volume, Postgres version, and rollback plans before production changes.
